refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct functional obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation choice) and focuses on the core problem: making spatial features available in a structured, reusable form. Atomicity is achieved by deferring the specific feature types (terrain, obstacles, zones) to sub-FRs.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that a worksite model exists in a defined schema/format, contains spatial coordinates and feature classifications, and is accessible to downstream planning and execution functions per the acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface properties across the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and locate static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent designated work zones (operational areas, restricted zones, safety perimeters) in the worksite model.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Persist and version the worksite model for access by planning and execution functions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data via multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety zone) queryable by planning/execution functions.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign feature capture responsibility to zone-specific acquisition modules; merge zone models via spatial registration and conflict resolution; expose unified model through a feature query interface.

DP3: Implement a real-time streaming pipeline that ingests raw sensor observations, applies incremental feature extraction and classification, and maintains a versioned spatial database; provide read-only snapshots and change notifications to downstream consumers.

DP4: Capture worksite features through manual annotation and structured data entry (forms, CAD import); store in a centralized feature registry with spatial indexing; synchronize with operational policies via a validation layer before exposure to planning/execution.

DP5: Establish a federated model where each safety/operational domain (e.g., hazard zones, equipment placement, access routes) maintains its own feature representation; provide a unified query facade that reconciles domain-specific models on demand.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Implement a real-time streaming pipeline that ingests raw sensor observations, applies incremental feature extraction and classification, and maintains a versioned spatial database; provide read-only snapshots and change notifications to downstream consumers.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between feature extraction logic and sensor modality—mitigated by abstracting sensor input as a normalized observation stream.
Dependency on external classification models (e.g., ML-based feature labeling)—managed via a pluggable classifier interface to avoid vendor lock-in.
Versioning and conflict resolution in the spatial database—addressed by explicit schema for temporal metadata and change provenance.
Synchronization with safety policies—decoupled via a separate validation/policy-enforcement layer that consumes the spatial model rather than embedding policy logic in the pipeline.
coupling_check.notes: DP3 achieves functional independence by separating concerns: acquisition (sensor abstraction), processing (feature extraction), storage (versioned database), and exposure (query + notification interfaces). The streaming architecture supports incremental updates and allows planning/execution to consume snapshots or subscribe to changes without blocking the capture pipeline. Coupling risks are manageable through well-defined interfaces.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Sensor data quality and availability are sufficient to extract and classify features reliably. Risk: Degraded sensor performance (occlusion, noise, calibration drift) may produce incomplete or incorrect spatial models; mitigation: implement confidence scoring and anomaly detection.

Assumption: Feature classification taxonomy is stable and agreed upon by safety/operations stakeholders. Risk: Misalignment between captured features and operational policy requirements may render the model unusable; mitigation: co-design taxonomy with stakeholders and version it explicitly.

Assumption: Real-time streaming is feasible given worksite scale and sensor bandwidth. Risk: High-frequency updates may overwhelm downstream consumers or introduce latency; mitigation: provide configurable snapshot intervals and change-notification thresholds.

Assumption: Spatial registration and coordinate frame alignment are solvable (e.g., via GNSS, fiducials, or visual odometry). Risk: Accumulated drift or initialization errors may degrade model accuracy over time; mitigation: implement periodic re-registration and uncertainty quantification.

Assumption: Planning/execution functions can tolerate eventual consistency (i.e., the spatial model may lag real-world state). Risk: Safety-critical decisions based on stale data may violate operational policies; mitigation: enforce maximum age bounds on model snapshots and alert consumers to data freshness.

Assumption: The worksite environment is sufficiently structured to enable feature extraction (e.g., distinct hazard zones, equipment, access routes). Risk: Highly dynamic or unstructured sites may produce ambiguous or unstable feature representations; mitigation: define feature extraction confidence thresholds and require manual validation for low-confidence detections.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP3 to explicitly separate the **acquisition layer** (sensor abstraction and normalization), **processing layer** (feature extraction and classification with confidence scoring), **storage layer** (versioned spatial database with temporal metadata), and **exposure layer** (read-only snapshots, change notifications, and policy-aware query interface). Add a **validation/policy-enforcement layer** that operates downstream of the spatial model to ensure captured features comply with worksite safety and operational policies before being made available to planning/execution. This layering clarifies module boundaries (IM) and reduces coupling between sensor technology, feature logic, and policy enforcement. Additionally, define explicit acceptance criteria for feature completeness, accuracy, and freshness in terms of operational requirements (e.g., 'all hazard zones must be detected with >95% confidence within 5 minutes of worksite entry').

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Real-time streaming pipeline encompassing sensor ingestion, incremental feature extraction, classification logic, versioned spatial database management, snapshot generation, and change notification distribution.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingests raw sensor observations from Vehicle and external sources; provides the primary data stream for the pipeline.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Delivers read-only snapshots and change notifications to downstream consumers (e.g., Teleoperation, Safety Services) for decision-making and reactive tasking.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits critical alerts derived from classification results when safety thresholds are breached; feeds into Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Manages distributed communication for snapshot delivery and change notifications to remote consumers; handles versioning and consistency.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural home for the entire real-time streaming pipeline. It owns sensor data processing, feature extraction, classification, and spatial database management. Snapshot and notification logic are core NGHE responsibilities. Minimal diffusion: only external interface contracts (Telemetry in, Tasking/SafetyAlert/Network Link out) cross boundaries.

Vehicle is a data source and actuator platform, not a processing hub. Placing the pipeline here would scatter feature extraction, classification, and database logic across vehicle firmware, creating tight coupling and poor separation of concerns. Pipeline logic would diffuse into teleoperation and safety services.

Teleoperation is a consumer of snapshots and notifications, not their producer. The pipeline logic (ingestion, feature extraction, classification, database) has no cohesion with remote operation control. Placing it here inverts the dependency and couples teleoperation to real-time processing internals.

Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert outputs and acts on them. The full pipeline (sensor ingestion, feature extraction, spatial database, versioning) is orthogonal to safety decision logic. Containment here would force safety services to own data processing infrastructure, violating separation of concerns.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The DP is a cohesive, self-contained data processing and management system that logically belongs in NGHE Solution. NGHE Solution is the designated platform for real-time perception, feature extraction, and spatial reasoning. The pipeline's outputs (snapshots, notifications, alerts) are consumed by downstream modules (Teleoperation, Safety Services, Vehicle) via well-defined interfaces. This containment preserves modularity and allows NGHE Solution to evolve its processing logic independently.
containment_risks: Risk: If snapshot or notification formats are tightly coupled to specific consumer needs (e.g., Teleoperation UI layout), changes in consumer requirements could force pipeline refactoring. Mitigation: Define snapshot and notification schemas as stable contracts in the Tasking interface.

Risk: Versioned spatial database may grow large; if Vehicle or Teleoperation modules cache snapshots locally, consistency becomes a concern. Mitigation: Enforce read-only semantics and version-aware subscription patterns via Network Link interface.

Risk: Real-time performance requirements (latency, throughput) may conflict with database consistency guarantees. Mitigation: Establish SLAs in the DP and validate them during implementation; consider eventual consistency for non-critical snapshots.

Risk: SafetyAlert outputs must be deterministic and auditable; if classification logic is opaque or non-deterministic, safety certification becomes difficult. Mitigation: Require formal specification of classification rules and traceability from alerts back to sensor observations.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR ('Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model') is directly satisfied by DP3's versioned spatial database and snapshot delivery mechanism. Acceptance criteria require that 'a worksite model/map is generated and available to planning/execution functions for an agreed set of site features.' The Tasking interface (read-only snapshots + change notifications) provides the delivery mechanism; the NGHE Solution module owns the database and snapshot generation logic. Verification can be grounded in: (1) snapshot schema compliance with agreed feature set, (2) latency/availability SLAs for snapshot delivery, (3) traceability of features from sensor observations through classification to database records.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution is the unambiguous owner of the pipeline (95% containment score). The module boundary encompasses sensor ingestion, feature extraction, classification, versioned database, and snapshot/notification generation—all cohesive responsibilities. External interfaces (Telemetry in; Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link out) are well-defined contract points. Downstream consumers (Teleoperation, Safety Services, Vehicle) are clearly identified as read-only snapshot/notification subscribers, not pipeline owners. No ambiguity in allocation.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly addresses the FR. The real-time streaming pipeline with incremental feature extraction, classification, and versioned spatial database is the logical solution to 'capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.' The DP specifies the mechanism (streaming ingestion, incremental processing, versioned storage) and the delivery contract (read-only snapshots, change notifications). This satisfies the acceptance criterion that the model 'is generated and available to planning/execution functions.' No independence violation; DP is neither over-constrained nor under-specified relative to the FR.

diffusion_risks: Snapshot/notification schema coupling: If Tasking interface schemas are tightly coupled to specific consumer implementations (e.g., Teleoperation UI layout, Safety Services alert format), changes in downstream requirements will force pipeline refactoring. Mitigation: Define snapshot and notification schemas as stable, versioned contracts in the Tasking interface specification; use schema evolution patterns (e.g., optional fields, deprecation timelines) to decouple consumers.

Classification logic auditability: SafetyAlert outputs depend on classification results. If classification logic is opaque, non-deterministic, or scattered across multiple code paths within NGHE Solution, safety certification and traceability become difficult. Mitigation: Centralize classification logic in a single, formally specified module within NGHE Solution; require deterministic execution and full traceability from sensor observations to alert decisions.

Versioned database consistency vs. real-time performance: The DP requires both versioning (for consistency and rollback) and real-time streaming (for low-latency snapshots). If database write contention or version management overhead causes latency spikes, snapshot delivery SLAs may be violated. Mitigation: Establish explicit SLAs for snapshot latency and database consistency guarantees; consider eventual consistency for non-critical snapshots and strong consistency for safety-critical features; validate during implementation.

Distributed snapshot caching: If Vehicle or Teleoperation modules cache snapshots locally for offline operation or performance, version skew and stale-data risks emerge. Mitigation: Enforce read-only semantics via Network Link interface; implement version-aware subscription patterns; document cache invalidation policies and version reconciliation procedures.

Feature extraction algorithm diffusion: If incremental feature extraction logic is duplicated across multiple code paths (e.g., real-time path vs. batch reprocessing path), maintenance and consistency risks arise. Mitigation: Implement feature extraction as a single, reusable module within NGHE Solution; use dependency injection or strategy patterns to support both real-time and batch modes without code duplication.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct and natural home for DP3. The module exhibits high cohesion (95% containment score): all pipeline responsibilities—sensor ingestion, feature extraction, classification, versioned database, snapshot generation, and change notification—are logically grouped and owned by a single module. This preserves separation of concerns: Vehicle is a data source, not a processor; Teleoperation and Safety Services are consumers, not producers. The external interfaces are clean contract points with minimal coupling. The only diffusion risks are at the DP<->IM bridge: snapshot/notification schema stability, classification logic auditability, and performance/consistency trade-offs. These are manageable through interface specification discipline and implementation validation, not architectural restructuring.

recommended_revisions: Formalize Tasking interface schema: Define snapshot and notification data structures as stable, versioned contracts. Include feature set enumeration, version identifiers, timestamp semantics, and change delta formats. Document schema evolution policy (e.g., backward compatibility guarantees, deprecation timelines) to prevent consumer coupling.

Specify classification logic requirements: Require formal specification of classification rules, decision thresholds, and determinism guarantees. Establish traceability from sensor observations through feature extraction to classification outputs to SafetyAlert emissions. Define audit logging and replay capabilities for safety certification.

Establish real-time SLAs: Define explicit latency, throughput, and consistency SLAs for the streaming pipeline. Specify snapshot delivery latency (e.g., <100ms for critical features), database write latency, and version consistency guarantees. Validate SLAs during implementation; document trade-offs (e.g., eventual consistency for non-critical snapshots).

Implement feature extraction as a reusable module: Centralize incremental feature extraction logic in a single, well-tested module within NGHE Solution. Support both real-time and batch modes via dependency injection or strategy patterns to avoid code duplication and maintenance drift.

Define Network Link versioning and consistency protocol: Specify how snapshot versions are tracked, how change notifications reference versions, and how consumers reconcile version skew (e.g., in case of network delays or local caching). Document version-aware subscription patterns and cache invalidation procedures.

Add safety certification requirements to DP3: Explicitly require that classification logic and SafetyAlert outputs meet safety standards (e.g., ISO 26262 for automotive, IEC 61508 for functional safety). Define determinism, failure modes, and diagnostic coverage requirements. Link to Safety Services interface contract.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence. The FR is well-defined, DP3 is a sound logical solution, and NGHE Solution is the correct architectural home. However, the gate analysis identifies six material diffusion risks at the DP<->IM bridge, all of which are safety-critical or performance-critical. These risks cannot be fully mitigated at the current level of specification; they require decomposition of DP3 into sub-level DPs and explicit IM ownership to ensure auditability, consistency, and safety certification. The high safety criticality of the worksite model (it feeds SafetyAlert outputs) and the complexity of the real-time streaming + versioning + classification pipeline justify drilling down to expose and resolve these risks before implementation. Stopping here would defer critical architectural decisions (schema stability, classification logic auditability, SLA validation) to implementation, increasing rework and certification risk.
next_level_sub_frs: FR3.1: Ingest and normalize sensor observations from Vehicle telemetry in real-time with lossless traceability to source sensors and timestamps.
FR3.2: Extract and incrementally compute spatial features (e.g., obstacles, terrain, landmarks) from normalized sensor observations with deterministic, auditable logic.
FR3.3: Classify extracted features into safety-critical and non-critical categories with formal specification, determinism guarantees, and full traceability for certification.

FR3.4: Maintain a versioned spatial database of classified features with strong consistency for safety-critical features and eventual consistency for non-critical features, supporting rollback and audit trails.

FR3.5: Generate and deliver read-only snapshots of the spatial database to downstream consumers (Teleoperation, Safety Services, Vehicle) via stable, versioned Tasking interface schemas with change notifications.
FR3.6: Enforce real-time performance SLAs (snapshot latency, database write latency, consistency guarantees) and validate compliance during operation with diagnostic logging.

revise.why: DP3 is logically sound but under-specified at the implementation bridge. The six diffusion risks (snapshot schema coupling, classification logic auditability, versioning vs. real-time performance trade-offs, distributed caching, feature extraction duplication, and safety certification) require explicit sub-level DPs to address. Revising DP3 to include these sub-DPs will clarify the internal architecture of NGHE Solution, establish ownership of critical sub-modules (e.g., Feature Extraction Module, Classification Module, Versioned Database Module, Snapshot/Notification Module), and define interface contracts (Tasking schema, SafetyAlert traceability) that prevent downstream coupling and enable safety certification. This revision is not a rejection of DP3 but a deepening of its specification to make the DP<->IM mapping explicit and verifiable.

assumptions: NGHE Solution is a single, cohesive module with 95% containment of the DP3 pipeline; no architectural restructuring is needed.
Downstream consumers (Teleoperation, Safety Services, Vehicle) are read-only subscribers to snapshots and notifications; they do not own or modify the spatial database.
The Tasking interface is the primary contract point for snapshot and notification delivery; it must be stable and versioned to prevent consumer coupling.
Classification logic is deterministic and auditable; non-determinism or opacity would violate safety certification requirements.
Real-time streaming and versioning are both required; trade-offs between latency and consistency must be explicitly managed via SLAs.
Feature extraction logic is centralized in a single module within NGHE Solution; duplication across code paths is not acceptable.
open_risks: Snapshot/notification schema coupling: Downstream consumers may tightly couple to specific schema versions, making schema evolution difficult. Mitigation: Define stable, versioned Tasking interface schemas with backward compatibility guarantees and deprecation timelines.

Classification logic auditability: If classification rules are opaque, scattered, or non-deterministic, safety certification and traceability become infeasible. Mitigation: Formalize classification logic as a single, auditable module with determinism guarantees and full traceability from sensor observations to SafetyAlert outputs.

Versioning vs. real-time performance: Database write contention or version management overhead may cause snapshot delivery latency to exceed SLAs. Mitigation: Establish explicit SLAs for snapshot latency and database consistency; validate during implementation; consider eventual consistency for non-critical features.

Distributed snapshot caching: Vehicle or Teleoperation modules may cache snapshots locally, creating version skew and stale-data risks. Mitigation: Enforce read-only semantics via Network Link interface; implement version-aware subscription patterns; document cache invalidation policies.

Feature extraction algorithm diffusion: Incremental feature extraction logic may be duplicated across real-time and batch code paths, creating maintenance and consistency risks. Mitigation: Implement feature extraction as a single, reusable module with dependency injection to support both modes.

Safety certification of classification logic: SafetyAlert outputs depend on classification results; if classification logic does not meet safety standards (e.g., ISO 26262, IEC 61508), certification is at risk. Mitigation: Explicitly require safety certification requirements in sub-level DPs; define determinism, failure modes, and diagnostic coverage.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of Tasking interface schemas (snapshot structure, notification format, version identifiers, change delta semantics) with schema evolution policy.

Formal specification of classification rules, decision thresholds, and determinism guarantees; traceability matrix from sensor observations through feature extraction to classification outputs to SafetyAlert emissions.
Real-time SLA specification: snapshot delivery latency (e.g., <100ms for critical features), database write latency, version consistency guarantees (strong vs. eventual), and validation plan.
Design of Feature Extraction Module: single, reusable implementation supporting both real-time and batch modes via dependency injection or strategy patterns; code review and test coverage evidence.

Design of Versioned Database Module: consistency model (strong for safety-critical, eventual for non-critical), write contention mitigation, rollback and audit trail capabilities, and performance validation.

Safety certification plan: mapping of classification logic to safety standards (ISO 26262, IEC 61508); determinism proof; failure mode analysis; diagnostic coverage; audit logging and replay capabilities.
Network Link versioning and consistency protocol: version tracking, change notification semantics, consumer version reconciliation procedures, and cache invalidation policies.
Implementation validation evidence: SLA compliance testing, schema stability testing, classification logic auditability testing, and safety certification audit results.
